data.title: An address deposited 5,000 ETH into Binance. Flap supports fire relief efforts in Hong Kong.
data.description:
Headline

▌An address deposited 5,000 ETH into Binance, bringing the total deposits over the past two weeks to over 13,400 ETH

According to on-chain analyst Ai Yi's monitoring, an address that bought WBTC at a low price four years ago and took profits at a high price this year deposited 5,000 ETH into Binance 10 hours ago, worth approximately $15.36 million. This address has cumulatively deposited 13,403.28 ETH into exchanges over the past two weeks, with a total value of $41.06 million. Currently, this address still holds 15,000 ETH and has interactions with addresses associated with GalaxyDigital; the ownership of the wallet cannot be confirmed at this time.

▌Flap Platform Meme Token Tax Mechanism Helps Animoca Hong Kong Fire Relief Fund Donate Over 76 BNB

Recently, on the Flap platform, a community-run Meme token ($Hong Kong Charity Fund) has donated over 76 BNB (approximately US$67,000) to the Hong Kong Relief Fund initiated by Animoca through its transaction tax mechanism. This donation was completed in less than a day. According to on-chain data, the tax generated from the Meme token transaction was donated to the Animoca charity address through the execution of a contract (address 0xE789548A54E8420DAa8977d27CD43fB6CEdE5D16). The on-chain transaction hash and data list can be viewed on the Dune platform. This case demonstrates a new model of spontaneous charitable donations by the crypto community through smart contract tax mechanisms.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is approximately $90,678.00, with a 24-hour change of approximately -0.17%; ETH price is approximately $3,028.57, with a 24-hour change of approximately +0.82%; BNB price is approximately $884.78, with a 24-hour change of approximately... 0);">-0.93%；

SOL price is approximately $137.14, with a 24-hour change of approximately -1.36%；

DOGE price is approximately $0.14974, with a 24-hour change of approximately -0.31%；

XRP price is approximately $2.18, with a 24-hour change of approximately -0.1%；

TRX price is approximately **$0.28227, 24-hour change approximately +1.22%;** **WLFI price is approximately $0.15946, 24-hour change approximately -0.44%.** **Policy** **Trump: Revokes All Documents Signed by Former President Biden via Automated Signing Device** **US President Trump:** All documents signed by “Sleepy Joe Biden” via automated signing devices (approximately 92% of his signed documents) are hereby revoked and permanently invalidated, effective immediately. Automated signature devices may not be used without the special approval of the US President. The radical left-wing lunatics surrounding the Resolute Desk in the Oval Office have stripped Biden of his presidential powers. I hereby rescind all executive orders and other documents not personally signed by Biden, due to the alleged illegal operation by the operators of the automated signature devices. Joe Biden did not participate in the automated signature process, and if he claims to have participated, he will face perjury charges. Please be aware!

▌The UK will strictly investigate crypto asset taxation; exchanges must report full user data from 2026

Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs (HMRC) has issued new regulations. From January 1, 2026, all cryptocurrency exchanges operating in the UK must collect and retain complete user transaction records and report them in full to HMRC by 2027 for comparison with tax information and to combat crypto asset tax evasion. British tax experts warn crypto users to finalize their tax situation by the end of 2026 to avoid potential sanctions.

Blockchain Applications

▌U.S. Bank is testing a custom stablecoin on the Stellar network

According to Crowdfundinside, U.S. Bank, PwC, and the Stellar Development Foundation (SDF) announced that U.S. Bank is testing the issuance of a custom stablecoin on the Stellar network.

▌São Paulo, Brazil to pilot blockchain-based microloans for small rural producers

Brazilian fintech company Tanssi is launching a government-backed blockchain project to provide microloans to small rural producers in São Paulo through a mobile application and physical payment machines.

This project uses blockchain infrastructure built by Tanssi, enabling predictable transaction fees and reliability, rather than relying on public blockchains like Ethereum or Solana, and is expected to launch next month. Cryptocurrency ▌Peter Schiff: Wall Street is still chasing cryptocurrencies, but a real bull market is on its way. Gold advocate and economist Peter Schiff writes that silver prices have just broken through $56, hitting a new all-time high. Gold prices have reached $4,220. Mining stocks are soaring, while Bitcoin is falling. Wall Street is still chasing cryptocurrencies, but a real bull market is on its way. BlackRock Address Receives 16,629 ETH and 300 BTC in the Last 10 Minutes According to Onchain Lens monitoring, in the past 10 minutes, BlackRock received 300 BTC, worth $27.51 million, and 16,629 ETH, worth $50.64 million, from Coinbase. Over the past three days, they have received 4,044 BTC worth $354 million and 80,121 ETH worth $235 million. Arthur Hayes maintains his prediction that Bitcoin will reach $250,000 by the end of the year, a rise of approximately 170%. Former BitMEX co-founder and CEO Arthur Hayes maintains his prediction that Bitcoin (BTC) will rise to $250,000 by the end of the year, an increase of about 170%. Hayes believes Bitcoin has bottomed out, with last week's drop to $80,600 marking the bottom, and it has since rebounded by about 12%. Hayes points out that the US liquidity tightening cycle is nearing its end, with the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in October. The market expects quantitative tightening (QT) to end as early as the beginning of December, with an 87% probability of another rate cut on December 10th. Coupled with the reset effect of the cryptocurrency market leverage liquidation on October 11th, this will provide upward momentum for Bitcoin. Although he acknowledges that his prediction may be inaccurate, he remains optimistic in the long term. **Important Economic Developments** S&P Dow Jones Indices: All Affected Indices Have Resumed Operation. S&P Dow Jones Indices stated that all affected indices have resumed operation. The Federal Reserve's overnight reverse repurchase agreements (RRPs) saw $7.561 billion used on Friday, compared to $2.217 billion in the previous trading session. Goldman Sachs traders say the U.S. stock market landscape has become clearer as we enter December. Goldman Sachs' trading arm says that while the S&P 500 may close November flat, the path forward has become significantly clearer as volatility eases, market breadth improves, and trend-following strategies shift to the buy side. Multiple indicators suggest the market is stabilizing as we enter December. Lee Coppersmith of Goldman Sachs wrote in a report to clients that market breadth, measured by the five-day moving average of the number of advancing S&P components minus the number of declining ones, fell to -150 earlier this month, indicating “quite significant damage beneath the surface.” However, the indicator rebounded to around +150 just before Thanksgiving. “This is a huge change – a broader participation, not just a narrow squeeze, and it further suggests that the market has released a significant portion of the pressure by mid-month,” he added. Goldman Sachs' volatility index conveys a similar message. The index is currently around 5, slightly above its three-year average and well below its early November high. Positions for so-called systematic strategies have also been reset. Goldman Sachs traders estimate that the sell-off related to the S&P 500 over the past month amounted to approximately $16 billion, stating that these transactions fueled the previous stock market decline. With this round of risk aversion largely priced in by the market, the bank's baseline expectation for next month is now for net buying of approximately $4.7 billion. "This means we are starting December with a much cleaner head than we were a few weeks ago," Coppersmith wrote.

▌US stocks closed three hours early due to the Thanksgiving holiday, with all three major indexes rising for the fifth consecutive trading day

US stocks closed three hours early due to the Thanksgiving holiday, with all three major indexes rising for the fifth consecutive trading day. The Nasdaq rose 0.65%, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.61%, and the S&P 500 rose 0.54%. The Nasdaq has risen 4.91% this week, the Dow Jones Industrial Average has risen 3.18%, and the S&P 500 has risen 3.73%. Most technology stocks rose, with Intel closing up 10%, its biggest one-day gain since September 18; Meta rose more than 2%, and AMD, Amazon, Netflix, and Microsoft all rose more than 1%.

...

▌ECB President: Interest Rates at Appropriate Levels

ECB President Christine Lagarde stated that current interest rates are within an appropriate range, and the ECB is fully prepared for policy changes. "In my view, the interest rate levels set at the previous meetings were appropriate," Lagarde said in an interview. "Given that the inflation cycle is under control, we remain in a favorable position." When discussing risks to the inflation outlook, she noted that the scope of risks has narrowed, but if the US imposes additional tariffs or supply chains are disrupted again, prices could face renewed upward pressure.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Artificial General Intelligence Really Think Like Humans?

When the lines between humans and machines become blurred, we see Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).

Unlike Artificial Intelligence in the Narrow Sense (ANI), which uses AI to solve single problems, AGI refers to AI that can understand, learn, and apply knowledge in a way that is difficult to distinguish from human cognition. AGI is still in the theoretical stage, but the prospect of AI fully replacing human input and judgment has naturally attracted widespread attention, and researchers, technology experts, and academics are working hard to turn the concept of AGI into reality. Although AGI is still in the theoretical stage, its enormous potential makes it a science fiction version of AI. While existing models (such as ChatGPT) are constantly being developed and improved, the journey to making AGI a reality still requires overcoming significant technical challenges. The question of whether general AI can think like a human delves into the core of human cognition. Human thinking is characterized by consciousness, emotional depth, creativity, and subjectivity. While general AI can simulate some aspects of human thinking, replicating the full range of human cognition is a formidable challenge. The touchstone of general AI lies in whether it can fully replicate human experience. Once achieved, its potential benefits will be immense, benefiting all walks of life and impacting every aspect of daily life. Despite its limitations, artificial general intelligence (AGI) is increasingly seen as a positive force across various industries. AGI could create computers as intelligent as humans, revolutionizing fields like cryptocurrency trading and market analysis. However, AGI requires trust and fairness to benefit everyone. Blockchain, the technology behind Bitcoin and Ethereum, offers a secure and transparent way to achieve this. However, persistent challenges such as slow blockchain speeds, crypto transaction latency, and limited storage capacity may hinder AGI from processing data quickly or handling large datasets. To prepare blockchain for AGI, researchers are exploring off-chain storage, sharding and danksharding, and data pruning. AGI represents the pinnacle of AI development, promising capabilities comparable to human intelligence. While AGI can simulate certain aspects of human thought, achieving truly human-like cognition remains a distant goal. Consciousness, emotional depth, and creativity are inherent properties of human experience and pose significant challenges to AGI. Nevertheless, the pursuit of general artificial intelligence continues to drive innovation and reshape our understanding of intelligence.

data.title: Is Circle poised to acquire South Korean payment provider? A "stablecoin defense war" erupts in South Korea.
data.description:
The traditional narrative of "de-dollarization" is being replayed in the blockchain world. Faced with the ambition of Circle (USDC), the world's second-largest stablecoin issuer, to "seize" the South Korean market, South Korean financial and technology giants have joined forces in a rare move, launching a "war to defend the Korean won stablecoin" aimed at safeguarding financial sovereignty. The "overt strategy" of dollar stablecoins: In August of this year, during his visit to South Korea, Circle President Heath Tarbert laid it all out to the four major financial institutions: "We have no intention of cooperating on Korean won stablecoins," and we will primarily promote dollar stablecoins. This isn't arrogance, but a shrewd "monopoly strategy": Seizing the vacuum: Circle attempts to rapidly capture DeFi and payment scenarios by leveraging the high liquidity of USDC during the window before the implementation of South Korea's Virtual Asset User Protection Act and stablecoin regulations. Creating a fait accompli: Once users develop the habit, local stablecoins will find it difficult to gain a foothold. This is precisely the nightmare of the Bank of Korea (BOK)—"Digital Dollarization" (i.e., the replacement of the domestic currency with digital dollars such as stablecoins). If South Koreans begin to get used to using USDC for savings and settlements, South Korea's monetary policy will become ineffective, and the unimpeded USDC channel will become a "highway" for capital outflow. This imminent threat to sovereignty directly spurred the emergency mobilization of South Korea's domestic "national team." The "Iron Triangle" Strategy – "Defense and Counterattack" Faced with the offensive from Silicon Valley giants, South Korea did not choose simple administrative sanctions, but instead launched a combined attack of "banks + games + Web3." [Banks] KakaoBank: Building a Compliance "Wall" South Korea's largest internet bank, KakaoBank, has officially entered the fray, developing infrastructure for a Korean won stablecoin. Strategic Significance: The biggest pain point for stablecoins is "trust." KakaoBank's entry means that the Korean Won stablecoin has gained the credit backing of the traditional financial system. It solves the core issue of fund custody, making the Korean Won stablecoin no longer an "air coin," but a digital 1:1 fiat currency mapping. [Games] WEMADE & CertiK: A "Nuclear Weapon" for Creating Application Scenarios If banks are the shield, then gaming giant WEMADE is the sword. By partnering with security giant CertiK to launch the GAKS alliance and the StableNet mainnet, WEMADE is doing something Circle struggles to do:

  • Consumer Monetization: South Korea is a global gaming hub. WEMADE boasts a massive player base, allowing them to directly embed the Korean Won stablecoin into the GameFi ecosystem.

  • A Disruptive Impact: When stablecoins become essential for trading game equipment and purchasing items, they transform from mere "speculative tools" into "high-frequency consumption currencies." [Web3] Diversified Rangers: Flanking Maneuvers Meanwhile, projects like KRWQ and KRW1 are experimenting with diversification on public chains such as Base and Avalanche. Although currently small in scale, they act like rangers, testing cross-chain technology and DeFi composability for the Korean Won stablecoin, ensuring South Korea doesn't lag behind in technological advancements. The Real "Ambition" of the Korean Won Stablecoin Some question, "South Korea's electronic payment system is already highly developed; why would it need a stablecoin to buy coffee?" This view seriously underestimates South Korea's strategic intentions. The goal of the Korean Won stablecoin has never been convenience stores, but the vast universe: Exporting "Game Financial Hegemony": Transforming South Korea's strong gaming industry advantages into financial competitiveness. Global players must use the Korean Won stablecoin for settlement when playing Korean games—this is an upgrade from cultural export to currency export. Regional Trade "De-SWIFTization": Bypassing the expensive and inefficient traditional SWIFT system, establishing a low-cost Asian clearing network with South Korea as the node.

Acquiring Offshore Funds: Rather than letting funds circulate aimlessly in uncontrollable offshore USD stablecoins, it's better to incorporate them into compliant on-chain systems, achieving "capital freedom within a regulatory framework."

The "Korean Template" of Asian Digital Finance

South Korea's "stablecoin defense war" provides a highly valuable example for all economies facing the impact of dollarization. It proves that in the Web3 era, the best way to maintain monetary sovereignty is not "total suppression," but rather "institutionalized absorption"—utilizing local technology and financial giants to build an ecosystem with a greater advantage than external competitors.

This defensive line jointly built by banks and gaming giants may be the prototype of the future Asian digital finance landscape.

This defensive line may be the prototype of the future Asian digital finance landscape.

2. Binance Wallet Adds Support for Monad Network

Jinse Finance reports that Binance Wallet has added support for the Monad network.

    3. Animoca Brands Executive: 2026 Will Expand Business Focus, Concentrating on Stablecoins, AI, and DePIN

    Jinse Finance reports that Keyvan Peymani, Chief Strategy Officer of Animoca Brands, stated that the company will expand beyond the gaming sector next year, further broadening its current portfolio of approximately 600 companies.

    ... In an interview with CNBC on Tuesday, Pemani stated, "We invest in dozens of companies every year, whether it's emerging fields like AI, DePIN, DeFi, and gaming, or stablecoins and other new opportunities. The core focus is on how the ecosystem we're in continues to expand." Gaming is the largest segment in Animoca Brands' portfolio—230 of its 628 invested companies are gaming-related. Founded in 2014, the company has been deeply involved in the gaming sector since its inception, with investments including Axie Infinity, Crypto Kitties, Colossal, and Yield Guild Games.

      4. South Korean authorities suspect North Korean hacker group Lazarus is behind yesterday's Upbit attack

      Golden Finance reported that the South Korean virtual asset exchange Upbit suffered a hacker attack, with virtual assets worth 44.5 billion won stolen.

      Currently, the Lazarus Group, a hacking group affiliated with the Reconnaissance General Bureau of North Korea, is highly suspected of being behind the incident. According to Yonhap News Agency, citing information and communication technology (ICT) industry sources and relevant South Korean government departments, authorities are conducting an on-site investigation of Upbit due to the high suspicion surrounding the Lazarus Group's involvement. This hacking group was previously accused of participating in the 2019 theft of 58 billion won worth of Ethereum from Upbit. A government official stated, "This attack is unlikely to have targeted the server; it's more likely that the administrator's account was stolen, or that someone impersonated an administrator to transfer funds." He added, "Based on the information we have, the hackers used the same method to carry out the attack six years ago, so we speculate that this is the same method used this time." According to Jinse Finance, Bitfury Group, a leading global cryptocurrency mining company, recently completed a $12 million strategic investment in Gonka.ai, a decentralized AI computing network, subscribing to 20 million GNK tokens at a price of $0.6 per token. Gonka.ai was founded by the Lieberman brothers, serial entrepreneurs, and aims to build a blockchain-based distributed GPU computing power market. This investment is Bitfury's first publicly disclosed investment since establishing a $1 billion ethical AI fund, indicating that traditional mining companies are systematically deploying in the decentralized AI infrastructure sector. Cryptocurrency mining company Bitfury invests $12 million strategically in decentralized AI computing network Gonka.ai, subscribing to 20 million GNK tokens at a price of $0.6 per token. As previously reported, Gonka completed an $18 million funding round in 2023, with investors including OpenAI investor Coatue Management, Solana investor Slow Ventures, K5, Insight, and several partners from Benchmark. Early builders also included several well-known industry institutions such as 6 Block, Hard Yaka, Gcore, and Hyperfusion. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: A multi-billion dollar acquisition was hacked the day after it was completed. Was it another North Korean hacker's doing?
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

South Korean cryptocurrency exchange Upbit disclosed that it detected abnormal withdrawals around 4 AM today. Approximately 44.5 billion won (about $30.43 million) of Solana network assets (including SOL, USDC, and a range of smaller tokens) were transferred to an unspecified external wallet. Upbit stated, "We immediately confirmed the outflow of assets due to the abnormal withdrawal and will use Upbit's assets to pay the full amount to ensure that user assets are not harmed."

Upbit has currently frozen approximately 2.3 billion won (about $1.57 million), and the remaining assets are being continuously tracked.

Following the incident, the exchange quickly blocked its infrastructure, transferred all assets to secure cold wallets to prevent unauthorized transfers, and conducted security audits on each wallet and signature system. Coincidentally, six years ago today, Upbit was also hacked. According to Cryptonews, that incident was attributed to North Korean hackers, with approximately $41.5 million worth of ETH stolen. Upbit also used its own funds to cover the entire amount and suspended trading for two weeks after the theft. Currently, Upbit states it is working with multiple projects and relevant institutions to attempt to further freeze or recover the stolen tokens and is preparing to hand over the information to law enforcement. According to South Korean media outlet BlockMedia, the Virtual Asset Supervisory Service of the Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) of South Korea has immediately launched an inspection of the platform. The FSA stated, "We are aware of the hacking incident and are currently investigating the details of the attack, the extent of the losses, and the measures taken to protect customer assets." Furthermore, according to Beosin Trace analysis, some funds that abnormally flowed out of Upbit have begun to be transferred. Binance user addresses (starting with 2zR) received SOL abnormally flowing out of Upbit from multiple intermediary addresses after the incident, currently receiving a total of approximately $315,000 worth of SOL. Additionally, Ki Young Ju, founder of Crypto Quant, also posted on the X platform, stating that after Upbit suspended withdrawals due to the hacking attack, arbitrage bots temporarily stopped functioning, allowing South Korean retail investors to take advantage of the situation and drive up the prices of various altcoins on the platform. Just days after announcing its merger, Upbit's parent company, Dunamu, suspended deposits and withdrawals due to asset theft. This comes after the company announced its merger with Naver Financial, valued at approximately $10.3 billion, making it one of the largest mergers in South Korean financial history. Besides promoting the Korean won's stablecoin and payment ecosystem, the deal also paves the way for Upbit's US IPO. Previous reports indicated that the two boards of directors would merge through an all-stock swap. Dunamu's share swap price was 439,252 won, while Naver Financial's share swap price was 172,780 won, a ratio of 1:2.54. Meanwhile, Dunamu's co-founders will hold approximately 30% of the merged shares, becoming the largest shareholder. Furthermore, to avoid violating South Korean antitrust regulations, Dunamu will delegate more than half of its voting rights to Naver, ensuring the merger structure can be successfully approved. Dunamu's recent financial report solidified its leading position among South Korean digital asset exchanges, with net revenue in the third quarter increasing by 300% year-on-year to $165 million, more than 300% higher than the same period last year. This financial performance provides a strong boost to the merger. This merger also demonstrates the high degree of complementarity between the two companies' businesses. As a top South Korean technology giant, Naver's business scope has expanded from its initial search engine to multiple fields such as e-commerce (Naver Shopping), payments (Naver Pay), and digital content (Naver Webtoon), forming a complete business ecosystem. Furthermore, with the launch of Dunamu's self-developed L2 GIWA Chain, it has expanded beyond its exchange business, transforming into a blockchain infrastructure provider that complements Naver's diversified business scenarios. In addition, this merger lays the foundation for a Korean won stablecoin; Dunamu's Korean won stablecoin will be issued primarily through Naver Pay, thus connecting the entire payment chain from the blockchain infrastructure to the user end. However, due to issues involving stablecoin risks, exchange compliance, and market competition, this transaction still requires review by the Korean Financial Supervisory Authority and the Fair Trade Commission. Moreover, in early November, Dunamu was fined approximately $25 million by the Korean Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) for KYC violations. Simultaneously, Upbit suspended new user registration and deposits/withdrawals for three months. South Korean regulators crack down on cryptocurrency exchanges, posing a challenge to Upbit's Nasdaq IPO. This is one of the heaviest fines ever levied against a cryptocurrency exchange in South Korea in recent years, and part of a broader enforcement campaign by the South Korean government to combat anti-money laundering and KYC violations in the cryptocurrency industry. The FIU stated that "during its anti-money laundering review of Dunamu, approximately 5.3 million KYC violations were discovered." The agency also noted that Dunamu failed to report 15 suspicious transactions. According to CoinDesk, Dunamu did not immediately plead guilty to the hefty fine and is even conducting an internal review and will appeal. A Dunamu spokesperson also emphasized that the FIU had made past errors in judgment. "The FIU previously fined Hanbitco 2 billion won for KYC deficiencies involving approximately 200 users, but the Seoul court subsequently overturned the fine, determining that the case did not constitute money laundering." However, this time the South Korean regulators did not back down, conducting thorough investigations of Dunamu, Korbit, GOPAX, Bithumb, and Coinone. According to the FIU report, in reviewing their anti-money laundering and other regulatory compliance, Bithumb, Coinone, Korbit, and GOPAX were also found to have violated multiple regulations. As South Korea's largest cryptocurrency exchange, Upbit's recent troubles, from the penalties it faced earlier this month to the theft of its assets, all occurred around the time Dunamu and Naver Financial announced their merger plan yesterday. This is particularly significant given the sensitive period following the merger, when Upbit is considering a Nasdaq IPO, undoubtedly posing a challenge to its expansion plans.

data.title: When Web3 Meets d/acc: What Can Crypto Do in an Era of Accelerated Technology?
data.description:
Author: imToken

If you followed the recent Devconnect conference in Argentina, you might have noticed an intriguing signal:

Among the numerous technical agendas regarding Rollups, EIPs, and account abstraction, the most eye-catching might not have been a protocol upgrade, but rather a dedicated day—d/acc day.

d/acc, which looks like an abbreviation of code symbols, is actually a new concept strongly promoted by Vitalik Buterin as early as 2023. This article will take you through a deep dive into the conceptual framework of d/acc and how Ethereum is accelerating the reshaping of its underlying narrative based on it.

I. Starting from e/acc, what is d/acc?

To understand d/acc, we must first understand the historical context it reflects: the frenzied pursuit of e/acc (effective accelerationism).

To understand d/acc, we must first understand the historical context it reflects: the frenzied pursuit of e/acc (effective accelerationism).

data.title: 5 Must-Read Articles for Evening | Predicting Market Supercycles
data.description:
1. Arthur Hayes: Why are Traditional Financial Derivatives Trading Becoming Perpetual Contracts?

Less than a decade ago, those self-proclaimed high-and-mighty traditional finance (TradFi) figures scoffed at BitMEX's 100x leveraged cryptocurrency derivatives market built using a new type of derivative contract called perpetual contracts. I couldn't help but laugh when I read articles by Jim Grant of Grant's Interest Rate Observer and Dan Oliver of Myrmikan Capital pointing out that BitMEX was offering such a "weapon of mass destruction" for cryptocurrency trading. However, things have changed. Suddenly, traditional exchanges like the Singapore Exchange (SGX) and the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) are also launching perpetual contracts or similar products by the end of 2025.

In the Pax Americana retail space, calls for both ordinary citizens and the elite to be able to trade perpetual contracts without a VPN prompted Coinbase to launch a "knock-off" version of perpetual contracts similar to the CBOE version earlier this year. Click to read

2. What are the two applications Vitalik endorsed? Is private communication the next big thing?

On November 27, 2025, Vitalik posted on X, endorsing two decentralized messaging applications, Session and SimpleX Chat, and donating 128 ETH to each.

... Click to Read

3. Dragonfly Partner: Why are New Public Chains Accompanied by Criticism from the Start?

I used to warn entrepreneurs: the feedback you get after your project launch won't be hatred, but indifference. By default, nobody cares about your new public chain. But now I have to stop saying that. Monad just launched this week, and I've never seen a newly launched blockchain suffer so much malice. I've been investing in the crypto space for over seven years. Before 2023, almost every new public chain I saw received either enthusiastic or indifferent feedback. But now, new public chains are accompanied by a barrage of criticism from the very beginning. Projects like Monad, Tempo, and MegaETH even faced a lot of verbal attacks before their mainnet launch—this is indeed a new phenomenon.

Click to Read

4. Tokenization of Equity in Non-Listed Companies: A Trillion-Dollar "Siege" and Attention Stealed by Perpetual Contracts

In the global asset landscape, equity in non-listed companies—especially high-growth unicorn companies—is an asset sector that combines both scale and imagination. However, for a long time, this growth potential has been almost entirely monopolized by professional institutions such as private equity (PE) and venture capital (VC), with only a few institutions and high-net-worth investors able to participate. Ordinary investors often can only watch the growth stories of unicorns in the news.

Click to Read

5. Predicting Market Supercycles

Prediction markets are not yet fully understood, and this is your opportunity. We are witnessing a complete reshaping of how markets operate, how information is priced, and most importantly—how the future is formed. If you ignore this, you will miss the biggest trading opportunity in the market since the digitalization of options. Click to Read

data.title: The collapse of stablecoin yields
data.description:
Foreword

Is the era of easily earning cryptocurrency yields truly over? A year ago, depositing money in stablecoins was like having a cheat code—generating high interest rates and (supposedly) no risk. Now, that illusion has shattered. Stablecoin yield opportunities across the entire cryptocurrency space have collapsed, leaving DeFi lenders and yield miners in a predicament of near-zero returns. Where did the once-golden goose of "risk-free" annualized yield (APY) go? And who should be held responsible for the decline of yield mining? Let's delve into the current state of stablecoin yields, and the results are shocking.

The Dream of "Risk-Free" Returns is Dead

Remember the good old days around 2021? Back then, various protocols were like candy vendors, enticing users to invest in USDC and DAI with double-digit annualized yields (APY).

Centralized platforms, promising stablecoin yields of 8% to 18%, grew their assets under management (AUM) to astonishing levels in less than a year. Even those DeFi protocols considered "conservative" offered stablecoin deposit yields exceeding 10%. It was as if we had hacked the financial system and struck gold! Retail investors flocked in, convinced they had found a 20% risk-free return on stablecoins. We all know how that ended. Fast forward to 2025: This dream is in jeopardy. Stablecoin yields have plummeted to single digits or even zero, utterly shattered by a double whammy of factors. The promise of "risk-free returns" has long since failed and was never truly real in the first place. DeFi's "golden goose" has become a headless chicken. Token crashes, yields collapse. The culprit is obvious: the cryptocurrency bear market. The drop in token prices has wiped out many sources of yield. The DeFi bull market was fueled by high-priced tokens; you could earn 8% stablecoin yields because protocols could mint and distribute governance tokens whose value skyrocketed. But when these token prices plummeted by 80% to 90%, the show was over. Liquidity mining rewards dried up or became virtually worthless. (For example, Curve's CRV token once approached $6, but now it's below $0.50—the plan to subsidize liquidity provider yields has completely failed.) In short, the bull market is over, and the free lunch is gone. Accompanying the price drop is a massive outflow of liquidity. The total value locked (TVL) in DeFi has evaporated from its peak. After peaking in late 2021, TVL began to plummet, dropping over 70% during the crash of 2022-2023. Billions of dollars fled protocols, either as investors cut their losses or as a chain reaction of failures forced projects to dissolve. With half the capital gone, yields naturally shrank dramatically: fewer borrowers, lower transaction fees, and significantly reduced token liquidity. The result: despite a slight rebound in 2024, DeFi TVL (more accurately, “total value loss”) has struggled to recover even a fraction of its former glory. When the fields turn to desert, yield farms yield nothing. Risk appetite? Extreme scarcity. Perhaps the most significant factor killing yields is fear. Risk appetite in the cryptocurrency market has plummeted. After the painful lessons of CeFi and the DeFi crash, even former speculators are saying “no, thank you.” Retail investors and whales alike have largely abandoned the once-popular game of chasing yields. Since the catastrophic events of 2022, most institutional funds have paused their cryptocurrency investments, and retail investors who suffered losses are now much more cautious. This shift in mentality is evident: why chase 7% yields on a lending app of dubious origin that could disappear overnight? The adage "If it's too good to be true, it's probably fake" has finally taken hold. Even within DeFi, users are only daring to choose the safest strategies. Leveraged yield mining, which swept through the DeFi summer, has now become a niche market. Yield aggregators and vaults are equally deserted; Yearn Finance is no longer a hot topic on CT. In short, nobody wants to try those fancy strategies anymore. The collective risk aversion is killing the once-attractive returns. No risk appetite = no risk premium. All that's left is a meager base interest rate. Don't forget the protocols themselves: DeFi platforms have also become more risk-averse. Many platforms have tightened collateral requirements, limited loan amounts, or closed unprofitable pools. Having witnessed competitors fail, protocols are no longer pursuing growth at all costs. This means less aggressive incentives and more conservative interest rate models, which again depress yields. The Traditional Finance Counterattack: With Treasury yields as high as 5%, why accept 3% yields in DeFi? Ironically, yields in traditional finance are starting to outperform cryptocurrencies. The US Federal Reserve's interest rate hikes in 2023-2024 pushed the risk-free rate (Treasury yield) to near 5%. Suddenly, the boring US Treasury bonds that grandmothers buy are outperforming a whole bunch of DeFi pools! This completely upends the game. The appeal of stablecoin lending lies in banks paying 0.1% interest, while DeFi pays 8%. But when government bonds offer a risk-free 5% yield, DeFi's single-digit returns seem unattractive after risk adjustment. Since government bond yields are higher, why would a sensible investor invest in a high-risk smart contract with only a 4% yield? In fact, this yield gap has led to a significant outflow of funds from the cryptocurrency space. Large investors are starting to invest in safe bonds or money market funds instead of stablecoin farms. Even stablecoin issuers can't ignore this trend; they're starting to invest their reserves in government bonds to earn substantial yields (most of which they keep for themselves). As a result, we're seeing large amounts of stablecoins sitting idle in wallets, untouched. The opportunity cost of holding stablecoins with a 0% yield becomes enormous, resulting in the loss of hundreds of billions of dollars in interest. Dollars held in "cash-only" stablecoin farms are doing nothing, while real-world interest rates are soaring. In short, traditional finance (TradFi) has stolen the thunder from decentralized finance (DeFi). DeFi yields must rise to compete, but without new demand, yields cannot rise. Therefore, funds can only flow out. Currently, Aave or Compound might offer around 4% annualized USDC yield (but with various risks), while the yield on a one-year US Treasury bond is similar or even higher. The harsh reality is: DeFi can no longer compete with TradFi after risk adjustment. Savvy investors understand this, and unless things change, funds won't flock back. Protocol Token Issuance: Unsustainable and Nearing its End Frankly, many attractive yields were never real to begin with. They either came from token inflation, venture capital subsidies, or were outright Ponzi schemes. This game can't last forever. By 2022, many protocols had to face reality: in a bear market, you can't keep paying 20% ​​annualized yields without collapsing. We've witnessed protocol after protocol slashing rewards or shutting down projects due to unsustainability. Liquidity mining activity has shrunk; as treasuries dry up, token incentives have been cut. Some yield farms can't even pay for token issuance—funds are drying up, and yield hunters are leaving. The yield farm boom is over. Protocols that once endlessly printed tokens are now grappling with the consequences (token prices plummeting, speculative capital long gone). In fact, the era of rolling yields is over. Crypto projects can no longer create currency out of thin air to attract users, or they risk destroying token value or incurring regulatory wrath. As fewer new "suckers" (ahem, investors) are willing to mine and sell tokens, the unsustainable feedback loop of yields collapses. The only remaining yields are those truly supported by real revenue (transaction fees, interest rate spreads), and even these are much smaller in scale. DeFi is being forced to mature, but in the process, its yields have shrunk to realistic levels. Yield Farming: A Ghost Town All these factors combined have made yield farms almost a ghost town. The once bustling mining farms and "speculative" strategies are now a distant memory. Browse cryptocurrency Twitter today; do you still see anyone boasting about 1000% annualized returns or new mining tokens? Not at all. Instead, you see exhausted veterans and those fleeing the liquidity crisis. The remaining yield opportunities are either minuscule and extremely risky (and therefore ignored by mainstream capital) or unbelievably low. Retail holders either leave stablecoins idle (with zero yield, but prioritizing security) or convert them to fiat currency and invest in off-chain money market funds. Whales are either busy closing deals to attract the attention of traditional financial institutions or simply holding dollars, showing no interest in participating in the yield game of decentralized finance (DeFi). The result: the DeFi "farm" is barren. DeFi is in winter, and crops are failing to grow. Even if yields still exist, the atmosphere is completely different. DeFi protocols are now focusing on connecting to real-world assets, squeezing out 5% here and 6% there. Essentially, they are building bridges with the traditional financial system—acknowledging that on-chain activity alone cannot generate competitive yields. The dream of a self-sufficient cryptocurrency yield universe is shattering. DeFi is gradually realizing that if you want "risk-free" returns, you'll eventually do what traditional finance (TradFi) does (buy government bonds or other physical assets). And guess what? Those yields are at best single digits. DeFi has lost its edge. So, the reality we face now is this: the era of stablecoin yields we know and love is over. A 20% annualized return is just a fantasy, and even 8% is gone forever. We have to face a harsh reality: if you want high returns in the cryptocurrency space now, you either have to take on enormous risks (and the corresponding risk of losing everything), or you're chasing illusory wealth. The average lending rate for DeFi stablecoins is even lower than bank time deposits. From a risk-adjusted perspective, DeFi yields are utterly laughable compared to other investment methods. There's no more free lunch in the crypto world. Let's be blunt, in typical doomsday rhetoric: the era of easily obtaining stablecoin yields is over. The dream of risk-free returns in DeFi didn't shatter out of thin air; it was strangled by a combination of factors, including market gravity, investor panic, competition from traditional finance, liquidity depletion, the unsustainability of token economic models, regulatory crackdowns, and harsh realities. Cryptocurrencies experienced a wild feast of returns, ultimately ending in tragedy. Today, survivors struggle to survive in the ruins, barely accepting a 4% yield and calling it a victory. Is the end of DeFi? Not necessarily. Innovation always brings new opportunities. But its essence has fundamentally changed. Cryptocurrency returns must be based on real value and risk, not on illusory internet currencies. The era of "stablecoin yields as high as 9% because of digital inflation" is over. DeFi is no longer a guaranteed profit for bank accounts; in fact, in many ways, it's even worse. A thought-provoking question: Will yield farms make a comeback? Or is it just a fleeting gimmick in the era of zero interest rates? Currently, the outlook seems bleak. Perhaps, if global interest rates fall again, DeFi could shine again by offering higher yields, but even then, trust has been severely damaged. Skepticism is hard to eradicate. The cryptocurrency community must now face a harsh reality: there is no such thing as a 10% risk-free return in DeFi. If you want high returns, you must invest your funds in highly volatile projects or complex schemes, which is precisely what stablecoins were originally designed to prevent. The initial intention of stablecoin yields was to provide a safe and rewarding haven. However, this illusion has been shattered. The market has finally realized that "stablecoin savings" is often a euphemism for playing with fire. Ultimately, perhaps this reflection is beneficial. Eliminating false yields and unsustainable promises may pave the way for more truly reasonable and reasonably priced investment opportunities. But this is only a long-term hope. The reality is harsh: stablecoins still promise stability, but no longer promise returns. The cryptocurrency mining market is shrinking, and many former "miners" have exited. DeFi, which once offered double-digit returns, now struggles to even match the returns of government bonds, and is far riskier. The market has noticed this, and it's voting with its feet (and its capital). As a critical observer, it's hard not to sharply question this: what's the point of a revolutionary financial movement if it can't even outperform your grandmother's bond portfolio? DeFi needs to answer this question, or the stablecoin slump will continue. The hype is over, the returns are gone, and perhaps even the tourists have left. What remains is an industry forced to confront its own limitations. Meanwhile, the notion of "risk-free returns" should also be abandoned. It once shone brightly, but now reality has returned; stablecoin returns are practically zero, and the cryptocurrency world must adapt to the post-pandemic era. Be prepared and don't believe any promises of easy profits. There's no such thing as a free lunch in this market. The sooner we accept this, the sooner we can rebuild trust, and perhaps one day we'll find rewards that are truly earned through our own efforts, rather than passively received.

data.title: Unbalanced market-making agreements are destroying potential projects.
data.description: Earlier this year, MOVE, the token of the Movement Foundation, plummeted nearly 20% after market makers dumped $38 million worth of shares, leaving retail investors deeply trapped. As the media provided detailed coverage of this collapse, various parties reacted swiftly: Coinbase immediately delisted the token, Binance froze funds, and the project founders distanced themselves from the situation. However, this was not an isolated incident, nor even a shocking scandal. This precisely exposes the deep-seated problems of the current ecosystem: Institutions responsible for providing liquidity wield excessive power; opaque lending protocols can destroy token prices; market makers are enriching themselves while investors remain unaware; crypto market makers are quietly determining the fate of early-stage projects in ways that are difficult for outsiders to detect. Ironically, in this world that touts decentralization, the most crucial levers for market functionality are often controlled by opaque and unbalanced protocols. These protocols deprive founders of bargaining power, allowing intermediaries to profit even if projects fail. Crypto market making needs transparent standards, better tools, and viable alternatives, but these advancements will never be realized if market makers continue to operate in the shadows. It's time to uncover the inner workings of market-making protocols. The Tragedy of "Loan + Call Option" Market Making Protocols The founders never anticipated the predicament they would face when they approached market makers. They were promised better liquidity, narrower bid-ask spreads, and more efficient price discovery, but what they often received were mispriced call options, distorted incentives, and inescapable structural disadvantages. This is not to accuse market makers of inherent evil. They are no different from other companies; having witnessed countless failed token launches (over 1.8 million this year alone), they have developed strategies to protect their own interests, regardless of whether the new token gains market acceptance or collapses quickly. The current mainstream market-making strategy is a "loan + call option" protocol. According to the protocol, projects lend their native tokens to market makers, who in turn commit to providing liquidity and maintaining market health through the buying and selling of tokens. Market makers simultaneously receive an option on the borrowed tokens, meaning they have the right (but not the obligation) to repay the token loan in cash if the token price surges. Theoretically, this is logically consistent: both parties share the benefits of price increases, and the market achieves stability. However, reality often contradicts this. These options are often aggressively mispriced: the strike price is set too high (sometimes 5-10 times the market price), and the vesting period is delayed. Market makers involved in drafting the protocol know full well that the probability of profiting from these options is extremely low. So they begin hedging, selling, and in some cases, even shorting the token. Their motivation is no longer to build a healthy market, but to relentlessly pursue risk-free profits, completely disregarding the survival of the projects they are supposed to support. The Unavoidable Choice for Most Projects The reason project teams accept these terms is simple: they have no other choice. While more favorable alternatives exist, project teams are required to provide both transaction tokens and stablecoin service fees. This prepayment model demands substantial financial reserves, which most teams cannot afford. After spending hundreds of thousands of dollars building offshore legal entities and compliance frameworks, project funds are insufficient to support both operations and liquidity. Founders are forced to resort to a less desirable solution: lending native tokens in exchange for liquidity, hoping it won't backfire. This situation is all too common. Some founders, eager to maintain the price of their tokens, may take more aggressive measures. They raise additional funds by using the platform's native token as collateral, and then use these funds to inflate their market capitalization. While this strategy can temporarily push up the price, it will inevitably trigger a sell-off when market maker options vest and expire. Retail investor confidence will collapse, the value of the national treasury will crumble, and the project team will ultimately wonder why they thought this approach was sustainable. Underlying this system is a profound information asymmetry. Market makers are derivatives experts, and founders are product builders. One party specializes in constructing asymmetric risk, while the other is often negotiating such transactions for the first time, with limited understanding of how these financial instruments perform under pressure. The result is predictable: unbalanced terms, hidden downside risks, and long-tail liabilities that only surface when things are beyond repair. Even more worrying is the complete lack of transparency and industry standards in the crypto market-making sector. There are no industry benchmarks, and no standardized disclosure mechanisms. Every protocol is a custom-made product, negotiated in the shadows and almost never publicly disclosed. Because crypto culture overemphasizes speed, teams race against time to launch products, and founders often only realize the damage is already deeply ingrained in the token economy. What the crypto industry needs is not just better protocols, but also a better framework for evaluating and understanding protocols. Each market-making protocol should be equipped with standardized disclosures: Option strike price, Hedging strategy, Incentive structure, and vesting plan. These disclosures must be made public to both the team and token holders. Founders need to understand the underlying benchmark models used to assess the fairness of protocols. Simulating outcomes under different market scenarios will reduce the number of people signing unclear terms. Early-stage teams should learn how market-making arrangements price, what risks they bear, and how to negotiate. Just as traditional companies wouldn't appoint a CFO unfamiliar with public offerings to handle an IPO, crypto projects shouldn't rashly issue tokens before fully understanding market-making mechanisms. In the long term, we need alternative liquidity solutions such as DAOs, liquidity pools, or trading platforms more aligned with founders' interests to eliminate the situation where huge returns are sacrificed for access to the underlying order book. These solutions require time to develop and won't happen overnight. But if the existing system remains unchallenged, they will never be born. At present, we can at least say it bluntly: the liquidity supply structure in the crypto space has collapsed. If not repaired in time, the core values ​​this space touts—fairness, decentralization, and user ownership—will ultimately continue to crumble in secretive protocols where no one dares to speak out.

data.title: Tokenization of Equity in Private Companies: Attention Stealing by Perpetual Contracts
data.description:
Author: Bitget Wallet

I. Introduction

In the global asset landscape, equity in unlisted companies—especially high-growth unicorn companies—is an asset sector that combines both scale and potential. However, for a long time, this growth potential has been almost entirely monopolized by professional institutions such as private equity (PE) and venture capital (VC), with only a few institutions and high-net-worth investors able to participate. Ordinary investors often can only watch the growth stories of unicorns in the news.

Blockchain and tokenization are changing this situation. By issuing tokens on-chain to represent equity in unlisted companies or their economic rights, the market hopes to build a new secondary market that can be traded 24/7 within a compliant framework, improving liquidity, lowering barriers to entry, and connecting TradFi and DeFi on a larger scale.

Institutions have also given this field extremely high expectations. For example, Citigroup believes that private equity tokenization could grow 80 times within ten years, approaching $4 trillion in size. Against this backdrop, the tokenization of equity in non-listed companies has naturally become one of the most watched sub-sectors in RWA. Its significance lies not only in technological innovation, but also in profound changes to asset participation mechanisms, exit methods, and profit structures. Bitget Wallet Research will take you through how equity tokenization will help non-listed companies break through this barrier in this article. II. A Trillion-Dollar "Siege": High Value, But Difficult to Enter and Exit From the asset side, equity in non-listed companies covers everything from startups to large private groups. Holders include founding teams, employee ESOPs/RSUs, angel investors, VC/PE funds, and some long-term institutions. From a funding perspective, according to publicly available data, global PE (private equity) assets under management are approaching $6 trillion, and VC (venture capital) assets under management are approximately $3 trillion, totaling about $8.9 trillion. Meanwhile, as of mid-2025, the total valuation of global unicorn companies hovered between $4.8 and $5.6 trillion, and this only represents the top few thousand companies at the very top of the pyramid; tens of thousands of mature private companies that haven't yet reached the "unicorn threshold" are not fully accounted for. Putting these figures together reveals a stark picture: a massive pool of assets worth trillions, yet a illiquid, walled city. On one hand, this market is inaccessible to the vast majority of people. Major jurisdictions generally limit primary private equity opportunities to a small circle of qualified and institutional investors, with minimum investment amounts often starting from hundreds of thousands or even millions of dollars. The combination of wealth and institutional barriers makes this asset class virtually untouchable for ordinary investors. On the other hand, those already inside the city often find it difficult to leave. For employees, angel investors, and VC/PE holders, the mainstream exit paths are almost exclusively IPO or M&A. Unicorn companies generally postpone their IPOs, with ten-year lock-up periods becoming the norm, making it difficult to realize paper wealth in the long term. While off-chain private equity secondary markets exist, they heavily rely on intermediaries, resulting in opaque processes, high costs, and long cycles, making them difficult to serve as large-scale liquidity outlets. The asymmetry between high-value assets and inefficient liquidity mechanisms provides a clear entry point for the tokenization of equity in non-listed companies—namely, reconstructing a new participation and exit path without disrupting regulatory and corporate governance order. III. What Does Tokenization Really Change? Under compliant conditions, the value of tokenization lies not only in moving equity onto the blockchain but also in reshaping three core mechanisms. First, continuous secondary liquidity. By tokenizing and splitting, high-value equity can be divided into smaller shares, allowing more compliant investors to participate in targets that were originally only for PE/VC with lower amounts. From the perspective of external investors, this is the starting point for ordinary people to buy some OpenAI/SpaceX; from the perspective of internal holders, it provides employees, early shareholders, and some LPs with a supplementary outlet besides IPO/M&A, enabling phased monetization in a 24/7 on-chain market under controllable thresholds. Secondly, it enables more continuous price discovery and market capitalization management. Traditional valuation of unlisted equity is highly dependent on financing rounds, with prices being discrete and lagging, even considered intermittent quotations. If, within a compliant framework, a portion of equity or economic rights is tokenized and put into continuous trading, the target company and primary investors can use more frequent market price signals to price subsequent financing, proactively managing market capitalization in a "quasi-public market" and bridging the valuation gap between primary and secondary markets. Finally, it provides new financing channels. For some high-growth companies, tokenization is not only a tool for transferring existing equity, but also a tool for issuing new capital. Through security token offerings (STOs) and other pathways, companies can potentially bypass expensive underwriting and lengthy IPO processes, directly raising funds from compliant global investors. This path is realistically attractive to companies that do not have short-term listing plans but wish to optimize their capital structure and improve employee mobility.

IV. Three Models: Real Equity on-chain, Mirror Derivatives, and SPV Structure

Regarding the topic of tokenization of equity in non-listed companies, there are currently roughly three implementation paths in the market, which differ fundamentally in their legal attributes, investor rights, and compliance paths.

The first type is the native collaborative model of real equity on-chain. In this model, the target company actively authorizes and participates, and equity registration, token issuance, and shareholder register maintenance are all completed within the regulatory framework.

On-chain tokens are legally equivalent to equity, granting holders full shareholder rights such as voting rights and dividend rights. A prime example is Securitize, which helped companies like Exodus and Curzio Research tokenize their equity, trade it on the ATS platform, and even list it on the NYSE. Its advantages lie in clear compliance and well-defined rights, but this requires strong cooperation from the issuer, resulting in a relatively slow implementation pace. The second type is synthetic mirror-image derivatives. These projects do not hold real equity; instead, they "index" the valuation of the target company through contracts/notes, then issue perpetual contracts or debt-type tokens. Investors legally form a debt or contractual relationship with the platform and are not registered as shareholders of the target company; their returns depend entirely on contract settlement. Ventuals is a representative of this model, leveraging Hyperliquid's perpetual contract infrastructure to break down the valuations of non-listed companies like OpenAI into tradable valuation units, allowing users to go long or short. The third type is the most common SPV indirect holding model in the current Crypto landscape. The issuing platform first establishes a special purpose vehicle (SPV), which acquires a small amount of equity in the target company in the traditional private secondary market. Then, the SPV's beneficial rights are tokenized and sold externally. Investors hold contractual economic beneficial rights to the SPV, not direct rights listed on the target company's shareholder register. The advantage of this model is its practicality; it can connect real equity with on-chain capital to some extent even without the issuer's cooperation. However, it also naturally faces dual pressure from regulatory agencies and the target company's legal department. Transfer restrictions in the shareholder agreement, the SPV's lack of transparency, and liquidation arrangements may all become points of contention in the future.

V. Derivatives Matching: When OpenAI is "On-Chained" by Perpetual Contracts

Recently, a new signal is reshaping the market's perception of Pre-IPO RWA: what many users actually want is not shareholder status, but the ability to bet on the rise and fall of unicorns like OpenAI and SpaceX at any time.

Hyperliquid has amplified this demand to the extreme.

Through the HIP-3 programmable perpetual contract layer, any team can create a new perp market by staking enough HYPE. To reduce the pressure of a cold start, Hyperliquid also launched Growth Mode, providing new markets with approximately 90% of taker fees waived, allowing long-tail assets to quickly accumulate depth and activity in the early stages. Just last week, Hyperliquid directly launched the OPENAI-USDH trading pair. This means that a company that is not yet listed and whose valuation is entirely dominated by the private market has been brought into a 24/7, leveraged, globally accessible on-chain market, creating a significant disruption to the pre-IPO RWA. The anticipated impact is very obvious. Pre-IPO equity tokens, lacking liquidity, are marginalized by the depth and speed of the perp market before they can truly mature. If this trend continues, the primary market may even have to refer to the on-chain price of the perp market for valuation, which will completely change the price discovery logic of private assets. Of course, the question arises: what exactly is the price of OPENAI-USDH anchored to? The market capitalization of unlisted companies doesn't have continuous off-chain pricing, but the on-chain perpetual contracts operate 24/7. This likely relies on a "soft anchoring" system built from oracles, long-term valuation expectations, funding rates, and market sentiment. For the Pre-IPO RWA sector, there are two real-world challenges: First, there's the squeeze on the demand side. When ordinary investors only want to bet on price and don't care about shareholder rights, dividends, or voting rights, perpetual contract DEXs based on Hyperliquid are often simpler, more liquid, and offer more leverage tools. In contrast, Pre-IPO equity tokenization products, if they only offer price exposure, will find it difficult to compete with perp DEXs in terms of user experience and efficiency. Second, there's the contrast between narrative and regulatory logic. Equity tokenization requires repeated adjustments and collaborations with regulatory agencies like the SEC and the issuer's legal system; while perp DEXs, currently operating in a regulatory gray area, have captured mindshare and trading volume with their lighter contract structure and global accessibility. For ordinary users, "first invest in perpetual contracts, then consider whether there is real equity" is becoming a more natural path. This doesn't mean the Pre-IPO RWA narrative is invalid, but it serves as a wake-up call. If this sector wants to go further, it must find its own differentiated positioning among "real shareholder rights, long-term capital allocation, cash flow distribution" and "on-chain native liquidity." VI. Conclusion: The Rewriting of Asset and Market Structures is Beginning. The importance of tokenizing equity in non-listed companies lies not in allowing more people to buy a piece of a unicorn, but in addressing the most fundamental pain points of private equity: high barriers to entry, narrow exit paths, and lagging price discovery. Tokenization has shown for the first time that these structural constraints can be redefined. In this process, Pre-IPO RWA is both an opportunity and a stress test. On the one hand, it reveals real needs—employees, early shareholders, and investors are all seeking more flexible ways to transfer assets; on the other hand, it also exposes real constraints such as regulatory friction, price anchoring, and insufficient market depth. Especially under the disruptive impact of perp DEX, the industry has more directly witnessed the speed and power of native on-chain liquidity. However, this does not mean that tokenization will stagnate. Changes in asset structure, transaction structure, and market structure often do not depend on a single model winning, but rather on issuers and infrastructure finding a sustainable compromise between regulation and efficiency. A hybrid path is more likely to emerge in the future, preserving shareholder rights and governance structures within a compliant framework while also ensuring continuous liquidity and global accessibility of the on-chain market. As more assets are listed on-chain in composable and tradable forms, the boundaries of unlisted equity will be redefined: it will no longer be a scarce asset in a closed market, but a liquid node in a global capital network.

data.title: South Korea Expands Crypto Travel Rule to Transactions Under $680 to Combat Money Laundering and Tax Evasion
data.description:
South Korea Expands Crypto Travel Rule to Cover Smaller Transactions

South Korea is tightening its grip on cryptocurrency oversight with plans to extend its Travel Rule to transactions as low as 1 million won, roughly US$680.

The announcement by Financial Services Commission Chairman Lee Eok-won at the 19th Anti-Money Laundering Day ceremony signals a stronger push against illicit financial activity in the digital asset space.

🚨JUST IN: SOUTH KOREA PLANS MAJOR CRYPTO AML OVERHAUL, WILL EXPAND TRAVEL RULE TO COVER TRANSACTIONS UNDER 1 MILLION KRW ($680) AND BLOCK HIGH-RISK OVERSEAS PLATFORMS pic.twitter.com/XikvplDbyg

— Blockchain Daily News (@blckchaindaily) November 28, 2025

How Will the Expanded Travel Rule Work

The Travel Rule, also known as the “cryptocurrency real-name system,” traditionally required Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) to share sender and receiver details for larger crypto transfers.

By lowering the threshold to 1 million won, South Korea aims to prevent criminals from splitting transactions into smaller amounts to evade detection.

Lee said during a National Assembly committee session,

“We will crack down on crypto money laundering […] expanding the Travel Rule to transactions under 1 million won.”

Alongside this, the FSC plans to block transactions with high-risk overseas exchanges, targeting platforms that pose an elevated money laundering risk.

Financial Services Commission Chairman Lee Eok-won at the National Assembly in Yeouido, Seoul, on 26 November.

The regulator will also increase scrutiny on VASP shareholders, assessing financial stability, criminal records, and social standing to prevent individuals with histories of drug offences or tax evasion from holding major positions in licensed firms.

Combating Tax Evasion and Cross-Border Schemes

Authorities highlight that the expanded regulations aim to curb the use of cryptocurrencies in tax evasion, drug trafficking, and other illicit overseas payment schemes.

Exchanges will undergo rigorous evaluations, and the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) will introduce pre-emptive account freezing measures for serious offences to prevent funds from disappearing during investigations.

These measures follow previous actions by South Korea’s National Tax Service, which in October announced it would conduct home searches and seize cold wallets or hard drives of users suspected of hiding crypto assets to avoid taxes.

🇰🇷 South Korea: Block Tax Alert 🚨

Tax agency escalates crypto evasion crackdown with home searches, cold wallet seizures, and blockchain tracking for delinquents.

Why it matters: Heightens global enforcement trends, could curb illicit use and align with FATF, but risks driving…

— Block Tax (@CryptoTaxG) October 11, 2025

The NTS also indicated it would analyse tax delinquent histories using specialised crypto-tracking software to identify potential offline concealment.

How Will Crypto Users and Exchanges Be Affected

Individual users and exchanges will face stricter reporting and verification requirements even for smaller transfers.

VASPs will need to enhance compliance systems to handle increased monitoring, while legitimate users may benefit from a safer trading environment with reduced illegal activity.

The regulations also signal a wider focus on ensuring investor protection and market transparency.

Challenges Ahead for Regulators and Exchanges

Implementing these expanded rules presents logistical and technological challenges.

Tracking a larger number of smaller transactions demands advanced systems and substantial resources, while exchanges must maintain user convenience alongside compliance.

Effective coordination between regulators, VASPs, and international authorities, such as the Financial Action Task Force, will be essential to enforce these measures successfully.

When Will the New Rules Take Effect

While Lee Eok-won unveiled the plans on 28 November, the FSC has yet to finalise implementation timelines.

Legislative amendments are expected to be submitted to the National Assembly in the first half of 2026, aligning with a broader strategy to strengthen anti-money laundering regulations following updates to the Special Financial Information Act in 2021.
